title: The kind of stranger we trust without thinking
summary: Describe a specific type of stranger that most people instinctively trust, and explain what signals or cues create that trust.
topic: The kind of stranger we trust without thinking
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) describing and explaining a type of stranger that people tend to trust quickly, without being fully conscious of why. Use at least one relative clause, a conditional sentence, the present perfect for observation, and 'however' or 'although' for concession.
hintStarters: There is a particular kind of person in a public place who makes you feel, almost immediately, that…
Most of us would say we are cautious around strangers, but the truth is we extend trust constantly, and often…
If you paid attention to the moments when you asked a stranger for help, you would probably notice that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: trao tin tưởng, mở lòng tin
cảm giác trực giác, linh cảm
làm ai đó cảm thấy thoải mái
trấn an, tạo cảm giác yên tâm

sampleText:
Think of the last time you asked a stranger for directions. You did not choose randomly — you scanned the street and picked someone, and you did it in about three seconds. The person you chose was probably not the most visually striking person there. They were likely standing still rather than rushing, facing outward rather than staring at a phone, and they had what I would describe as an air of calm — a presence that suggested they were not preoccupied with something urgent. That calm is the main signal that most people read, often without realising they are reading anything at all.

Although we tend to think of trust as something we reason our way into, researchers who have studied social behaviour in cities like Amsterdam and Nairobi have found that the initial decision to approach a stranger takes less than four seconds and relies almost entirely on non-verbal signals. The stranger who appears trustworthy is usually one who makes brief, neutral eye contact — not staring, just acknowledging — and who holds their body in a way that is open rather than closed. They do not lean away; they do not turn their shoulder. If you observed a crowd and marked the people others approached for help, you would find a consistent pattern: these people look as though they belong exactly where they are and have no particular urgency about moving somewhere else. That belonging, that lack of hurry, is deeply reassuring to a stranger who needs a small favour. It says: I have a moment, and I am not a threat.

What is interesting is that this kind of trust has very little to do with age, dress, or appearance in the way people assume. I have watched elderly women in Lisbon ask teenagers in hooded jackets for help because those teenagers happened to be sitting quietly rather than moving fast. I have seen business travellers in Tokyo approach street vendors rather than other suits. The cue is almost always the same: stillness and a willingness to be present. What we have essentially built, across many different cultures, is a shared shorthand for safety — one that most of us have never been taught but learned anyway, simply by being around other people long enough.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: scanned the street and picked someone
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: quan sát đường phố và chọn ai đó
không bận tâm về điều gì cấp bách

sampleAnnotations.phrase: without realising they are reading anything at all
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: mà không nhận ra rằng họ đang đọc bất kỳ tín hiệu gì
dùng lý trí để đi đến kết luận

sampleAnnotations.phrase: relies almost entirely on non-verbal signals
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: phụ thuộc gần như hoàn toàn vào tín hiệu phi ngôn ngữ
ánh mắt ngắn và trung lập
họ không ngả người ra sau

sampleAnnotations.phrase: look as though they belong exactly where they are
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: trông như thể họ thuộc về đúng nơi đó
rất trấn an, tạo cảm giác yên tâm sâu sắc
một ân huệ nhỏ, sự giúp đỡ nhỏ
tình cờ đang ngồi yên lặng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: stillness and a willingness to be present
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: sự tĩnh lặng và sẵn sàng hiện diện
ký hiệu ngầm chung về sự an toàn
vẫn học được, học theo cách tự nhiên

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for observed patterns
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If you observed a crowd and marked the people others approached for help, you would find a consistent pattern' dùng quá khứ đơn trong mệnh đề 'if' và 'would + động từ nguyên thể' trong mệnh đề chính — đây là điều kiện loại 2, miêu tả kết quả giả định nếu bạn thực hiện một hành động cụ thể.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If you observed a crowd and marked the people others approached for help, you would find a consistent pattern' uses past simple in the if-clause and 'would + base verb' in the main clause — a second conditional describing a hypothetical outcome if you performed a specific action.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for accumulated observation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What we have essentially built, across many different cultures, is a shared shorthand for safety' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn đạt một kết quả đã được tích lũy theo thời gian — không phải một sự kiện đơn lẻ trong quá khứ, mà là quá trình hình thành qua nhiều thế hệ và nền văn hóa.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What we have essentially built, across many different cultures, is a shared shorthand for safety' uses the present perfect to express an outcome accumulated over time — not a single past event, but a process formed across generations and cultures.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hãy nghĩ lại lần cuối bạn hỏi đường một người lạ. Bạn không chọn ngẫu nhiên — bạn quan sát đường phố và chọn ai đó, và bạn làm điều đó trong khoảng ba giây. Người bạn chọn có lẽ không phải là người nổi bật nhất ở đó. Họ có khả năng đang đứng yên thay vì vội vã, nhìn ra phía ngoài thay vì nhìn chằm chằm vào điện thoại, và họ có điều tôi mô tả là vẻ bình thản — một sự hiện diện cho thấy họ không bận tâm về điều gì cấp bách. Sự bình thản đó là tín hiệu chính mà hầu hết mọi người đọc được, thường mà không nhận ra rằng họ đang đọc bất kỳ điều gì cả.

Mặc dù chúng ta có xu hướng nghĩ rằng niềm tin là thứ chúng ta suy lý mà đi đến, các nhà nghiên cứu đã nghiên cứu hành vi xã hội ở các thành phố như Amsterdam và Nairobi đã phát hiện rằng quyết định ban đầu để tiếp cận một người lạ mất chưa đến bốn giây và phụ thuộc gần như hoàn toàn vào các tín hiệu phi ngôn ngữ. Người lạ có vẻ đáng tin thường là người tạo giao tiếp bằng mắt ngắn và trung lập — không nhìn chằm chằm, chỉ thừa nhận — và giữ cơ thể theo cách mở thay vì khép kín. Họ không ngả người ra sau; họ không quay vai đi. Nếu bạn quan sát một đám đông và đánh dấu những người mà người khác tiếp cận để nhờ giúp đỡ, bạn sẽ thấy một mô hình nhất quán: những người này trông như thể họ thuộc về đúng nơi họ đang ở và không có gì cấp bách về việc di chuyển đến nơi khác. Sự thuộc về đó, sự thiếu vội vàng đó, rất trấn an đối với một người lạ cần sự giúp đỡ nhỏ. Nó nói lên rằng: tôi có một chút thời gian, và tôi không phải mối đe dọa.

Điều thú vị là loại tin tưởng này ít liên quan đến tuổi tác, trang phục hay ngoại hình theo cách mọi người giả định. Tôi đã thấy những phụ nữ lớn tuổi ở Lisbon nhờ thanh thiếu niên mặc áo khoác có mũ chỉ đường vì những thanh thiếu niên đó tình cờ đang ngồi yên lặng thay vì di chuyển nhanh. Tôi đã thấy những người du lịch công tác ở Tokyo tiếp cận người bán hàng rong thay vì những người mặc vest khác. Tín hiệu hầu như luôn giống nhau: sự tĩnh lặng và sẵn sàng hiện diện. Về cơ bản, điều chúng ta đã xây dựng, qua nhiều nền văn hóa khác nhau, là một ký hiệu ngầm chung về sự an toàn — một ký hiệu mà hầu hết chúng ta chưa bao giờ được dạy nhưng vẫn học được, đơn giản là vì đã ở gần người khác đủ lâu.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly to describe a person?
options: The stranger which appears trustworthy is one which makes brief, neutral eye contact.
The stranger who appears trustworthy is one who he makes brief, neutral eye contact.
The stranger who appears trustworthy is one making brief, neutral eye contact who.
The stranger who appears trustworthy is one who makes brief, neutral eye contact.
mcQuestions.explanation: Use 'who' for people in relative clauses, and do not repeat the subject with a pronoun like 'he'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Who' introduces a relative clause about a person; don't add 'he' or 'she' inside the clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: If you observed a crowd, you would find a consistent pattern.
If you observed a crowd, you will find a consistent pattern.
If you observe a crowd, you would find a consistent pattern.
If you observed a crowd, you would found a consistent pattern.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple ('observed') in the if-clause + 'would + base verb' ('would find') in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Past simple in the if-clause, 'would' + bare infinitive in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'although' correctly to introduce a concession?
options: Although we tend to think of trust as something we reason into but researchers have found otherwise.
Although we tend to think of trust as something we reason into, researchers have found otherwise.
Although, we tend to think of trust as something, researchers have found otherwise.
We tend to think of trust although researchers have found otherwise about something.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no extra 'but', no comma after 'although'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' introduces a concessive clause directly, without 'but' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly for an accumulated observation?
options: What we have essentially build is a shared shorthand for safety.
What we essentially built is a shared shorthand for safety.
What we have essentially built is a shared shorthand for safety.
What we have essentially been build is a shared shorthand for safety.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = 'have/has + past participle'; 'built' is the past participle of 'build'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Build' is irregular: past participle is 'built', not 'build'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly uses 'rely on' in a sentence?
options: The decision relies almost entirely in non-verbal signals.
The decision relies almost entirely at non-verbal signals.
The decision relies almost entirely for non-verbal signals.
The decision relies almost entirely on non-verbal signals.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Rely on' is the correct preposition collocation; 'in', 'at', and 'for' are all incorrect here.
mcQuestions.hint: The verb 'rely' always pairs with the preposition 'on'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'as though' correctly to describe appearance?
options: They look as though they belong exactly where they are.
They look as though they are belonging exactly where they are.
They look as though they belonged exactly where are they.
They look as though they would belong exactly where they are.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Look as though + present simple' describes how someone appears right now; no need for continuous or conditional forms.
mcQuestions.hint: 'As though' + present simple describes a current appearance or impression.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'happen to + infinitive' for an unplanned action?
options: Those teenagers happened to sitting quietly rather than moving fast.
Those teenagers happened to be sitting quietly rather than moving fast.
Those teenagers happened being sitting quietly rather than moving fast.
Those teenagers were happening to sit quietly rather than moving fast.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Happen to + base verb' (or continuous after 'happened to be') expresses an unplanned circumstance; past tense is 'happened to be + -ing'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Happen to be + -ing' describes an unplanned ongoing action in the past.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'rather than' to show a contrast?
options: They look outward rather than to stare at a phone.
They look outward rather staring at a phone.
They look outward rather than staring at a phone.
They look outward, but rather than they stare at a phone.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Rather than + gerund' is the most natural structure when contrasting two actions.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Rather than' is followed by a gerund (-ing) when contrasting two actions.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a modal verb correctly to soften a description?
options: They had what I will describe as an air of calm.
They had what I describe would as an air of calm.
They had what I am describing as an air of calm.
They had what I would describe as an air of calm.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What I would describe as' uses 'would' to soften or qualify a description — a common B1 hedging phrase.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Would describe' softens the statement; use it before 'as' to introduce a characterisation.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'little to do with' to dismiss a connection?
options: This kind of trust has very little to do with age or appearance.
This kind of trust has very little doing with age or appearance.
This kind of trust has very little to do age or appearance.
This kind of trust has very little do with age or appearance.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have little/nothing to do with' is a fixed phrase meaning 'not related to'; the infinitive 'to do' is required.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase is 'have little to do with' — 'to do' is an infinitive here, not a gerund.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly introduces a contrasting example with 'rather than'?
options: Business travellers approached street vendors rather than approaching other suits.
Business travellers approached street vendors rather than other suits.
Business travellers approached street vendors, rather not other suits.
Business travellers rather than approached street vendors, not other suits.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both 'rather than + gerund' and 'rather than + base form' are acceptable; option A uses the base form cleanly.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'rather than', use a parallel verb form — base form or gerund, but be consistent.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If the signals we use to judge strangers are learned and cultural rather than universal, does it mean we should trust our gut feeling less — or simply understand it better?
